comment: Auto-populated by scripts/enrich-catalog.js. Each record carries source-cited structured data per book (characters/items/plot_beats/specific_facts/unknown_fields). The quiz generator + QC enforce a closed-list citation contract against this data when a record exists. Run `node scripts/enrich-catalog.js` to populate.

record.premise: A rhyming picture book without one continuous plot. The narrator walks the reader through a parade of short rhyming scenes — first about lots of different fish, then about a long cast of made-up creatures whose names rhyme with what they do, what they wear, or what they own. The book celebrates rhyme, opposites, counting, and noticing funny things everywhere.

characters.name: The child narrator(s)
characters.role: Unnamed narrator(s) who speak in first person ('I' or 'we') and guide the reader through the book

characters.name: Mr. Gump
characters.role: A man who owns a seven-hump Wump

characters.name: Ned
characters.role: A small character whose little bed is too short for him

characters.name: Mike
characters.role: A creature who sits in the back of a bike made for three and pushes it up hills

characters.name: Joe
characters.role: Someone who is being telephoned

characters.name: The Wump
characters.role: A humped creature you can ride; the narrator's Wump has one hump, Mr. Gump's has seven humps

characters.name: The Nook
characters.role: A creature with a hook on his head holding a cookbook; can't read so can't cook

characters.name: The Zans
characters.role: A creature whose job is to open cans

characters.name: The Gox
characters.role: A creature the narrator boxes with

characters.name: The Ying
characters.role: A creature that sings

characters.name: The Yink
characters.role: A creature that likes to wink and drinks pink ink

characters.name: The Yop
characters.role: A creature that hops from finger top to finger top

characters.name: The Zeds
characters.role: Small yellow pets with one hair on top of each head; need a haircut every day

characters.name: Ish
characters.role: A character with a dish in his hand that he uses to wish for fish

characters.name: The Gack
characters.role: A creature used in a backyard game called 'Ring the Gack'

characters.name: Clark
characters.role: A creature found in the park in the dark and taken home

characters.name: Zeep
characters.role: A pet that is taken to bed at the end of the day

key_objects.detail: Carried by one fish
Driven by another fish
Worn by the fat fish
Mike pushes it from the back up hills
Too small for Ned; his feet stick out
On the Nook's head, holding the cookbook 'How to Cook'
Opened by the Zans
Worn by the narrator while boxing the Gox
Worn by the Gox (visible in illustration)
Drunk by the Yink
Used by Ish to wish for fish
Chewed through by a mouse

plot_beats.beat: The book opens with the famous rhyme: 'One fish, two fish, red fish, blue fish' followed by descriptions of different fish (black, blue, old, new, sad, glad, bad, thin, fat)
One fish has a little star; another has a little car; the fat fish has a yellow hat
A creature with eleven fingers is shown and counted
The narrator rides a one-hump Wump and meets Mr. Gump with his seven-hump Wump
Ned is introduced; he doesn't like his little bed because his feet stick out, and a cow, dog, cat, and mouse all pile into the bed with him
A bird is found inside someone's ear
Mike sits at the back of a bike made for three and does the work pushing it up the hills
The Nook is shown with his hook and cookbook, unable to read or cook
By moonlight, sheep are seen walking in their sleep
The Zans opens lots of cans
The narrator boxes the Gox while wearing yellow socks
The narrator sings along with the Ying
The Yink drinks pink ink and likes to wink
The Yop hops from finger tip to finger tip
A pet with blue hair is described as fun to brush and comb
The Zeds (yellow, one hair, daily haircut) appear
A child walks past with ten cats on her head
Someone tries to phone Joe, but a mouse has cut the wire
Ish uses his dish to wish for fish
The backyard game 'Ring the Gack' is played
Clark is found in the park in the dark and taken home
At bedtime the narrator sleeps with the pet Zeep
The book closes with: 'Today is gone. Today was fun. Tomorrow is another one.' and the idea that funny things are everywhere

record.setting: A bright, made-up world. Scenes move quickly between the children's home and bedroom, a backyard, a park at night, hills where a bike has to be pushed, and open imaginary space where each new creature appears. There is no single fixed location.
themes: The fun of rhyme and reading aloud

Some fish are thin, some are fat

Riding the Wump sounds like 'Bump! Bump! Bump!'
Ned's bed is too small; his feet stick out
Animals that pile into Ned's bed: cow, dog, cat, mouse
A bird is found inside someone's ear
The bike is made for three; Mike sits in the back and does the work uphill
The Nook has a hook on his head holding the cookbook 'How to Cook'
The Nook can't read, so he can't cook
Sheep are seen walking in their sleep by moonlight
The Zans opens cans
The narrator boxes the Gox wearing yellow socks; the Gox wears yellow boxing gloves
The Yink drinks pink ink and likes to wink
The Yop hops from finger top to finger top
A pet has blue hair that is fun to brush and comb
The Zeds are yellow, have one hair on their heads, and need a haircut every day
A girl walks past with ten cats on her head
A mouse cuts the phone wire when someone is trying to call Joe
Ish uses a dish to wish for fish
The backyard game is called 'Ring the Gack'
Clark is found in the park in the dark
The bedtime pet at the end is Zeep
Closing rhyme: 'Today is gone. Today was fun. Tomorrow is another one.'
The book closes with the idea that funny things are everywhere

unknown_fields: names_of_child_narrators (the book does not name them; web sources claim 'Jay and Kay' from a different book but the authoritative source confirms they are unnamed in this book)
number_of_cats_bows_color (web source mentions pink bows but not confirmed by authoritative source)

record.premise: Eight-year-old Jack and his seven-year-old sister Annie discover a magic tree house in the woods that transports them back in time to the age of dinosaurs, where they have adventures and find a mysterious gold medallion.

characters.name: Jack
characters.role: Main character, eight-year-old boy, Annie's older brother

characters.name: Annie
characters.role: Main character, seven-year-old girl, Jack's younger sister

characters.name: Pteranodon
characters.role: Friendly flying dinosaur who helps Jack escape from the T-Rex

characters.name: Triceratops
characters.role: Peaceful dinosaur the children encounter

characters.name: Anatosaurus
characters.role: Duck-billed dinosaur the children encounter

characters.name: T-Rex
characters.role: Fearsome dinosaur who chases Jack

key_objects.detail: Located high in an oak tree, full of books
Used to climb up to the tree house
Jack picks it up and wishes to see a dinosaur
Has the letter M engraved on it

plot_beats.beat: Jack and Annie are walking in the woods near their home when Annie spots a tree house high in an oak tree
They climb up to the tree house using a rope ladder and find it full of books
Jack picks up a book about dinosaurs and wishes he could see one
The tree house starts to spin and transports them to a prehistoric world
They meet friendly dinosaurs including a Pteranodon, Triceratops, and Anatosaurus
A fearsome T-Rex chases Jack
The Pteranodon helps Jack escape by carrying him through the air back to the tree house
Jack and Annie find a gold medallion with the letter M engraved on it
They wish to go home and the tree house spins again, returning them to Frog Creek
Jack keeps the medallion as proof of their adventure

record.setting: The town of Frog Creek, Pennsylvania, and a prehistoric world with dinosaurs

specific_facts.fact: Jack is eight years old
Annie is seven years old
Annie is Jack's younger sister
Jack and Annie live in Frog Creek, Pennsylvania
The tree house is in an oak tree
The gold medallion has the letter M engraved on it
The Pteranodon is described as a friendly herbivore
The Triceratops is described as peaceful
The Anatosaurus is described as duck-billed
Hardly any time has passed at home when they return
Jack keeps the medallion as proof of their adventure

unknown_fields: identity of the mysterious owner of the tree house (only hinted with letter M)
what grade Jack and Annie are in at school
how long they spend in the dinosaur world
what other books are in the tree house
what Jack and Annie look like (hair color, clothing)
time of day when they find the tree house
season when the story takes place

characters.name: Pig

characters.name: Cow
characters.role: Farm animal the goose runs past

characters.name: Sheep
characters.role: Farm animal the goose runs past

plot_beats.beat: A goose escapes from its pen on a farm
A boy chases the goose with a broom
The goose runs past the pig, the cow, the sheep, and the duck
The animals all watch the chase
The goose is too fast for the boy to catch
The boy gets clever and uses corn to lead the goose

record.premise: A small rabbit named Peter ignores his mother's warning and sneaks into a gardener's forbidden garden, where he eats too many vegetables, is chased, loses his clothes, and only just escapes home — ending up sick in bed while his obedient sisters enjoy a proper supper.

characters.name: Peter Rabbit
characters.role: The naughty young rabbit protagonist who disobeys his mother and sneaks into Mr. McGregor's garden

characters.name: Flopsy
characters.role: One of Peter's three obedient sisters

characters.name: Mopsy
characters.role: One of Peter's three obedient sisters

characters.name: Cotton-tail
characters.role: One of Peter's three obedient sisters

characters.name: Mrs. Rabbit
characters.role: Peter's mother who warns the children and cares for Peter when he returns home

characters.name: Peter's father
characters.role: Only mentioned; he had an accident in Mr. McGregor's garden and was put in a pie by Mrs. McGregor

characters.name: Mr. McGregor
characters.role: The gardener who chases Peter through the garden

characters.name: Mrs. McGregor
characters.role: Mr. McGregor's wife; mentioned only as the one who put Peter's father in a pie

characters.name: Friendly sparrows
characters.role: Small birds who fly down to Peter when he is stuck in the net and implore him to exert himself

characters.name: A white cat
characters.role: Peter sees it staring at some goldfish in a pond; he walks away without speaking to it

characters.name: An old mouse
characters.role: Running in and out carrying peas and beans to her family; Peter asks her the way to the gate but she cannot answer because she has a large pea in her mouth

key_objects.detail: Blue with brass buttons, described as 'quite new'; gets caught in the gooseberry net and ends up on the scarecrow
One lost among the cabbages, one among the potatoes

key_objects.item: A loaf of brown bread and five currant buns
key_objects.detail: What Mrs. Rabbit buys at the baker's
Where Peter and Mr. McGregor first meet
Where Peter gets stuck because of his jacket buttons
Mr. McGregor's tool for trying to catch Peter
Peter's hiding place in the tool shed; it has water in it
Peter climbs on it to see the gate
Mr. McGregor hangs Peter's jacket and shoes on it to frighten blackbirds
Peter's bedtime medicine, one tablespoonful

plot_beats.beat: Mrs. Rabbit warns Flopsy, Mopsy, Cotton-tail, and Peter not to go into Mr. McGregor's garden because their father had an accident there and was put in a pie by Mrs. McGregor
Mrs. Rabbit goes through the wood to the baker's and buys a loaf of brown bread and five currant buns
Flopsy, Mopsy, and Cotton-tail go off to gather blackberries
Peter disobeys and runs straight to Mr. McGregor's garden, squeezing under the gate
Peter eats lettuces, then French beans, then radishes; feeling sick, he looks for parsley
Round the end of a cucumber frame, Peter meets Mr. McGregor, who chases him shouting 'Stop thief!'
Peter loses one shoe among the cabbages and the other shoe amongst the potatoes
Peter gets stuck in a gooseberry net when the large buttons on his jacket catch
Friendly sparrows fly to Peter and beg him to exert himself
Mr. McGregor comes with a sieve to catch Peter, but Peter wriggles out just in time, leaving his jacket behind
Peter rushes into a tool shed and jumps into a watering can that has water in it
Peter sneezes ('Kertyschoo!') and Mr. McGregor begins searching the shed
Peter jumps out a window, upsetting three plants; Mr. McGregor cannot fit through and goes back to work
Peter sees a white cat staring at goldfish by a pond and decides to walk away without speaking to it
Peter climbs onto a wheelbarrow and spots the gate beyond Mr. McGregor
Peter runs as fast as he can and slips underneath the gate to safety
Mr. McGregor hangs the lost jacket and shoes on a scare-crow to frighten the blackbirds
Peter runs home and flops down; his mother wonders what he has done with his clothes — it is the second jacket and shoes he has lost in a fortnight
Peter is not well; Mrs. Rabbit puts him to bed and gives him camomile tea — one tablespoonful at bedtime
Flopsy, Mopsy, and Cotton-tail have bread and milk and blackberries for supper

record.setting: The countryside. The rabbits live in a sand-bank, underneath the root of a very big fir tree. Most of the story takes place in Mr. McGregor's garden, which contains rows of vegetables, a cucumber frame, gooseberry nets, a tool shed, and a gate.
themes: Family contrast between obedient and naughty children

specific_facts.fact: The four rabbit children's names in order: Flopsy, Mopsy, Cotton-tail, and Peter
The rabbit family lives in a sand-bank, underneath the root of a very big fir tree
Peter wears a blue jacket with brass buttons (described as 'quite new') and shoes
Peter's father was put in a pie by Mrs. McGregor
Mrs. Rabbit buys a loaf of brown bread and five currant buns at the baker's
The three good sisters gather blackberries
Peter eats lettuces, French beans, and radishes, in that order
Peter looks for parsley after eating too much
Peter meets Mr. McGregor round the end of a cucumber frame
Mr. McGregor shouts 'Stop thief!'
Peter loses one shoe among the cabbages and the other shoe among the potatoes
Peter gets stuck in a gooseberry net
The large brass buttons on Peter's jacket get caught in the net
Sparrows encourage Peter to exert himself
Mr. McGregor tries to trap Peter with a sieve
Peter hides inside a watering can that has water in it
Peter sneezes 'Kertyschoo!' which gives him away
Peter upsets three plants when jumping out the window
Peter sees a white cat staring at goldfish by a pond
Peter climbs on a wheelbarrow to see the gate
Peter escapes by slipping underneath the gate
Mr. McGregor hangs Peter's clothes on a scare-crow to frighten blackbirds

Mrs. Rabbit gives Peter camomile tea — one tablespoonful at bedtime
Flopsy, Mopsy, and Cotton-tail have bread and milk and blackberries for supper

season when the story takes place

record.premise: Five gentle, slightly silly short stories about a sweet and slightly anxious Owl who lives by himself in a small house. Owl gets into small misadventures — letting Winter inside, being afraid of the bumps at the bottom of his bed, making tea out of tears, trying to be in two places at once, and befriending the moon.

characters.name: Owl
characters.role: Main character who lives alone in a small house; appears in every story as the gentle, worried hero

characters.name: Winter
characters.role: A guest in the first story who knocks on the door and is invited in; treated as a character who can knock, be invited in, and be shouted out

characters.name: The Moon
characters.role: Owl's friend in the last story; treated as a character rather than just scenery

key_objects.detail: In Owl's hearth; important in The Guest and Strange Bumps
What Owl serves Winter
What Owl serves Winter; Winter freezes it into hard green ice
The bed Owl jumps on and that crashes down in Strange Bumps
Owl cries into it to make tear-water tea
Has twenty steps; Owl runs up and down it in Upstairs and Downstairs
Where Owl finally sits down to rest

key_objects.item: The large rock at the seashore
key_objects.detail: Where Owl meets the moon
Where the moon shines in at the very end

plot_beats.beat: Story 1 (The Guest): Owl is at home by his fire when something knocks at the door; he invites Winter in
Story 1: Owl serves Winter buttered toast and hot pea soup
Story 1: Winter blows out the fire, whirls snow up the stairs, makes window shades flap, and turns the pea soup into hard green ice
Story 1: Owl shouts at Winter to leave; Winter rushes out and slams the door
Story 2 (Strange Bumps): In bed at night, Owl notices two strange bumps at the bottom of his bed under the blanket
Story 2: Owl tries to get rid of the bumps by pulling off covers, moving his feet, and jumping on the bed until the bed crashes down
Story 2: Frightened, Owl goes downstairs and sleeps in his chair near the fire; the bumps were his own two feet
Story 3 (Tear-Water Tea): Owl puts a kettle on his lap and thinks of sad things until he cries enough tears to fill it
Story 3: Owl boils the kettle and drinks his tea, saying it tastes a little bit salty
Story 4 (Upstairs and Downstairs): Owl tries to be upstairs and downstairs at the same time by running up and down his twenty-step staircase
Story 4: Tired out, Owl sits down on the tenth step (the middle) and rests
Story 5 (Owl and the Moon): Owl sits on a large rock at the seashore and talks to the moon, saying they must be very good friends
Story 5: A cloud covers the moon and Owl feels sad, thinking the moon has gone away
Story 5: Owl walks home; the moon shines through his bedroom window and Owl falls asleep no longer feeling sad

record.setting: Owl's own house — a cosy home with a fire, a chair, a bed upstairs, a staircase, a kettle and a pea-soup pot. One story also takes place at the seashore, on a large rock, looking out at the moon.
themes: Being on your own and quiet solitary domestic life
Feelings — being scared, sad, lonely, and satisfied
Accepting you can't be in two places at once

specific_facts.fact: The book contains five short stories
The five story titles in order are: The Guest, Strange Bumps, Tear-Water Tea, Upstairs and Downstairs, Owl and the Moon
Owl lives by himself
In The Guest, the visitor Owl invites in is Winter
Owl serves Winter buttered toast and hot pea soup
Winter turns the pea soup into hard green ice
Winter blows out the fire, whirls snow up the stairs, and slams the door when he leaves
In Strange Bumps, Owl sees two strange bumps at the bottom of his bed
Owl is afraid the bumps will grow bigger in the night
The bumps are actually Owl's own two feet
After his bed crashes, Owl sleeps in his chair near the fire downstairs
In Tear-Water Tea, Owl makes tea by crying into a kettle

Sad things Owl thinks about include: chairs with broken legs, forgotten songs, spoons behind the stove, books with torn pages, stopped clocks, mornings nobody saw, uneaten mashed potatoes, and pencils too short to use
Owl says the tear-water tea tastes a little bit salty
In Upstairs and Downstairs, Owl's staircase has twenty steps
Owl wants to be upstairs and downstairs at the same time
Owl rests on the tenth step, which is the middle
In Owl and the Moon, Owl is sitting on a large rock at the seashore
Owl says to the moon: We must be very good friends
When a cloud covers the moon, Owl feels sad
At the very end, the moon shines through Owl's bedroom window and Owl falls asleep
Owl says it is always a little sad to say goodbye to a friend

Whether Owl has any family members
The exact color of Owl's feathers or clothing

record.premise: Five gentle short stories about two friends — Frog and Toad — who do everything together even though they have very different temperaments. Frog is usually calm, helpful, and patient; Toad is grumpy, anxious, and easily worried. Each story is built around something simple in their friendship: waking up in spring, telling a story, losing a button, going for a swim, and waiting for the mail.

characters.name: Frog
characters.role: Main character; the taller, greener, more cheerful of the two friends. Patient, kind, and a fixer of problems.

characters.name: Toad
characters.role: Main character; the shorter, browner, grumpier friend. Worries, gets cross, and is the more emotional of the pair.

characters.name: Sparrow
characters.role: Minor character who appears in A Lost Button; hands Toad a wrong button (two-holed).

characters.name: Raccoon
characters.role: Minor character who appears in A Lost Button; hands Toad a wrong button (square).

characters.name: Turtle
characters.role: Minor character who appears in A Swim; watches Toad from the riverbank.

characters.name: Lizards
characters.role: Minor characters who appear in A Swim; watch Toad from the riverbank.

characters.name: Snake
characters.role: Minor character who appears in A Swim; watches Toad from the riverbank.

characters.name: Dragonflies
characters.role: Minor characters who appear in A Swim; watch Toad from the riverbank.

characters.name: Field Mouse
characters.role: Minor character who appears in A Swim; watches Toad from the riverbank.

characters.name: Snail
characters.role: Minor character in The Letter; Frog asks Snail to carry the letter to Toad.

key_objects.detail: Frog tears off the pages for November, December, January, February, March, and April to trick Toad into thinking it is May.
Loses a button; later ends up covered in wrong buttons and given to Frog as a present.
White, big, round, thick, with four holes; eventually found on Toad's own floor.
The source of all the embarrassment in A Swim; Toad thinks he looks funny in it.
Short letter from Frog to Toad, signed 'Your best friend, Frog.'
Toad's mailbox is always empty until the snail finally arrives.

plot_beats.beat: Story 1 (Spring): Frog wakes up on a sunny spring morning and runs to Toad's house to wake him up.
Toad refuses to get up and tells Frog to come back and wake him up at about half past May.
Frog tears off six calendar pages (November, December, January, February, March, and April) so Toad will think it is already May.
Toad gets up and they go outside together.
Story 2 (The Story): Frog is not feeling well; Toad says he looks quite green.
Toad tries hard to think of a story: he walks up and down the porch, stands on his head, pours a glass of water over his head, and bangs his head against the wall.
Toad cannot think of a story and goes to bed feeling unwell. Frog is feeling better and tells Toad a story instead.
Story 3 (A Lost Button): Frog and Toad go on a long walk across a meadow, through the woods, and along the river.
When they get home, Toad realizes a button has come off his jacket. He describes it as white, big, round, thick, and four-holed.
They retrace their walk. Frog finds a black button, Sparrow brings a two-holed button, Frog finds a small button, Raccoon brings a square button, and Frog finds a thin button — all wrong.
Toad gets crosser and crosser, throws the wrong buttons up in the air, and stomps home.
Toad finds his real button lying on the floor of his house.
Toad feels guilty for shouting, sews all the wrong buttons onto his jacket, and gives the jacket to Frog as a present.
Story 4 (A Swim): Frog and Toad go to the river for a swim. Toad puts on a bathing suit and thinks he looks funny in it.
While Toad is in the water, animals arrive on the bank one by one: Turtle, Lizards, Snake, Dragonflies, Field Mouse — all curious to see Toad in his bathing suit.
Toad stays in the water a long time and gets cold. When he finally gets out, all the animals and Frog laugh at him because he does look funny.
Toad picks up his clothes and goes home.
Story 5 (The Letter): Toad sits sadly on the front porch because he has never had any mail — his mailbox is always empty.
Frog runs home and writes Toad a letter, then gives it to a snail to deliver.
Frog goes back and sits on Toad's porch with him to wait. Frog tells Toad what the letter says: 'Dear Toad, I am glad that you are my best friend. Your best friend, Frog.'
Toad says it is a very good letter. They sit on the porch together feeling happy.
Four days later the snail arrives at Toad's door with the letter. Toad is very pleased to have it.

record.setting: A gentle, illustrated world of small woodland houses, porches, paths through a meadow, woods, and a river. Frog and Toad each live in their own small house, and they visit each other often. The setting is calm, rural, and out of any specific time.

specific_facts.fact: The book has five short stories.
Story titles, in order: Spring, The Story, A Lost Button, A Swim, The Letter.
The two main friends are Frog and Toad.
In Spring, Toad wants to keep sleeping until 'half past May.'
Frog tricks Toad by tearing off six calendar pages: November, December, January, February, March, and April.
In The Story, Frog feels ill and Toad says he looks 'quite green.'
Four things Toad does trying to think of a story: walks up and down the porch, stands on his head, pours a glass of water over his head, bangs his head against the wall.
At the end of The Story, Frog tells Toad a story (not Toad telling Frog).
In A Lost Button, Toad's missing button is: white, big, round, thick, and has four holes.
The order of wrong buttons found: black (by Frog), two-holed (by Sparrow), small (by Frog), square (by Raccoon), thin (by Frog).
Toad finally finds his real button on the floor of his own house.
Toad sews all the wrong buttons on a jacket and gives the jacket to Frog.
In A Swim, Frog and Toad swim in the river.
Toad is embarrassed because he thinks he looks funny in his bathing suit.
Animals who come to look at Toad, in order: Turtle, Lizards, Snake, Dragonflies, Field Mouse.
At the end of A Swim, everyone laughs at Toad — including Frog.
In The Letter, Toad is sad because his mailbox is always empty.
Frog asks a snail to deliver his letter.
The snail takes four days to deliver the letter.
The text of Frog's letter: 'Dear Toad, I am glad that you are my best friend. Your best friend, Frog.'
Frog and Toad sit on the porch together feeling happy while waiting for the letter.
Frog is taller and green; Toad is shorter and brown.

record.premise: A young boy who calls himself Nate the Great is a self-styled detective. His friend Annie loses a picture she has painted, and Nate takes the case. He questions everyone who has seen the picture, follows his clues, and — between pancake breaks — works out what happened to it.

characters.name: Nate the Great
characters.role: The boy detective and narrator who talks in short, serious sentences as if he were a grown-up detective. He works alone and loves pancakes.

characters.name: Annie
characters.role: Nate's friend who has painted a picture and lost it. Her whole bedroom is yellow and she likes the color yellow.

characters.name: Fang
characters.role: Annie's big yellow dog who buries things (including bones) in the yard.

characters.name: Rosamond
characters.role: Annie's friend, described as strange. She has four cats.

characters.name: Super Hex
characters.role: One of Rosamond's four cats.

characters.name: Big Hex
characters.role: One of Rosamond's four cats.

characters.name: Plain Hex
characters.role: One of Rosamond's four cats.

characters.name: Little Hex
characters.role: One of Rosamond's four cats.

characters.name: Harry
characters.role: Annie's little brother who has been painting his own pictures using red paint.

key_objects.detail: Nate's signature food. He eats them at the start and is offered more by Annie.
Painted in yellow by Annie; a picture of her dog Fang.
The cause of the mystery.
Harry's painted-over version, which solves the case.
The clue that Annie paints in yellow. Has yellow walls, yellow bed, yellow chair, and yellow desk.
Where Fang buries bones, dug up looking for the picture.

plot_beats.beat: Nate is eating breakfast at home: pancakes, juice, pancakes, milk, and pancakes.
Annie phones Nate for help because she has lost a picture she painted.
Nate walks over to Annie's house and announces: 'Now I, Nate the Great, will find a lost picture.'
Annie tells Nate the picture is of her dog Fang, which she painted yesterday.
Nate sees Annie's room, which is big with a yellow bed, yellow chair, yellow desk, and yellow walls. He concludes Annie likes yellow.
Nate notices Fang is a big yellow dog who buries things in the yard and wonders if Fang buried the picture.
Nate and Annie dig up the yard and find buried bones, but no picture.

Nate and Annie go to Rosamond's house. Rosamond has four cats all named with 'Hex' in their names: Super Hex, Big Hex, Plain Hex, and Little Hex. Nate decides Rosamond is strange but did not take the picture.
Back at Annie's, Nate questions her little brother Harry, who has been painting his own pictures with red paint, including a red monster.
Nate looks at Harry's painting and realizes yellow and red make orange. Harry painted over Annie's yellow picture of Fang with red paint, turning it into an orange monster.
Annie is upset at first that her picture has been painted over, but she forgives Harry. Nate has solved another case.

record.setting: The neighbourhood where Nate, Annie, and their friend Rosamond all live. The story moves between three houses (Nate's house, Annie's house, Rosamond's house) and the yard outside Annie's. There are no exact place names.

Nate's favorite food is pancakes.

Annie has lost a picture she painted.
The picture is of Annie's dog Fang.
Fang is a big yellow dog.
Fang buries things (including bones) in the yard.
Nate and Annie dig in the yard looking for the picture.
They find bones when they dig, but not the picture.
Annie's bedroom has yellow walls, a yellow bed, a yellow chair, and a yellow desk.
Nate concludes that Annie likes yellow.
Rosamond is described as strange.
Rosamond has four cats.
All four cats have 'Hex' in their names.
The four cats' names are Super Hex, Big Hex, Plain Hex, and Little Hex.
Harry is Annie's little brother.
Harry has been painting his own pictures with red paint.
Harry painted what he calls a red monster.
Yellow + red = orange is the key color fact that solves the mystery.
Harry painted over Annie's yellow picture with red paint, turning it into an orange monster.
Annie is upset at first but forgives Harry.
Nate's catchphrase in narration is 'I, Nate the Great...'

record.premise: A lonely little boy named Henry has no brothers or sisters and no other children on his street. His parents agree he can have a dog. Henry picks out a puppy he calls Mudge, and Mudge grows into an enormous 180-pound dog. The book follows them from picking the puppy out, through Mudge growing up, through the day Mudge gets lost and Henry has to look for him.

characters.name: Henry
characters.role: Main character, a small lonely boy who is an only child with no other children on his street

characters.name: Mudge
characters.role: Henry's dog, starts as a puppy and grows to 180 pounds with straight fur and floppy ears

characters.name: Henry's parents
characters.role: Minor characters who eventually say yes to Henry getting a dog

key_objects.detail: The collars Mudge grows out of as he grows up
A recurring detail about Mudge

key_objects.item: Mudge's floppy ears and straight fur
key_objects.detail: The features Henry was looking for in a dog

plot_beats.beat: Henry is lonely because he has no brothers, no sisters, and no other children live on his street
Henry asks his parents for a dog, and after a while they say yes
Henry looks for a dog — he does not want curly hair or pointy ears
Henry finds a dog with straight fur and floppy ears and names him Mudge
Mudge grows very fast and goes through seven collars before reaching full size
Mudge grows to 180 pounds and about three feet tall
Henry and Mudge walk to school together every day, and Henry is no longer scared or lonely
Henry worries while at school that Mudge will not be there when he gets home
One day Mudge wanders off and gets lost
Both Henry and Mudge are sad while they are apart
Henry and Mudge find each other and are reunited
Henry hugs Mudge and promises never to go off without him again; they are best friends

record.setting: Henry's neighbourhood — a street where Henry is the only child. The action moves between Henry's house and yard, the walk Henry and Mudge take together each day, and the wider neighbourhood where Mudge wanders off. The setting is small, suburban, and gentle.

No other children live on Henry's street

Henry did not want a dog with pointy ears

Mudge is about three feet tall when fully grown
Mudge drools

Mudge gets lost and wanders off
Mudge feels sad when he is lost, just like Henry
Henry promises never to leave Mudge again after they reunite
Mudge becomes Henry's best friend
The book has seven short chapters

record.premise: A short picture book about a girl named Vashti who is sure she cannot draw. Her art teacher gently encourages her to just make a mark and see where it takes her. From one small dot, Vashti grows into a real artist and at the end passes the same lesson on to another child who thinks he cannot draw either.

characters.name: Vashti
characters.role: Main character; a girl who believes at first that she cannot draw but becomes a confident artist through the book

characters.name: The art teacher
characters.role: A patient, calm teacher (never named) whose simple words drive the whole story

characters.name: A little boy
characters.role: Appears at the end; admires Vashti's art and tells her he cannot draw either; becomes the next 'Vashti' in the story's cycle

key_objects.detail: Appears at the start in front of Vashti and again at the end when she gives one to the boy
What Vashti uses to make her first dot
The seed of the whole story; Vashti's first mark
Hangs Vashti's signed dot above the teacher's desk
Small, large, multi-coloured, overlapping, and the 'dot without a dot'
His first mark, drawn with a trembling pencil

plot_beats.beat: Art class is over. Everyone has finished but Vashti is still sitting at her desk with a blank sheet of paper in front of her.
Vashti tells her teacher 'I just can't draw!'
The teacher smiles and says 'Just make a mark and see where it takes you.'
Vashti grabs a marker and jabs at the paper, making a small, sharp dot.
The teacher pushes the paper back and says 'Now sign it.' Vashti thinks this is silly but signs her name in the corner.
The following week, Vashti comes back to art class and sees her signed dot hanging in a gold frame above the teacher's desk.
Looking at her framed dot, Vashti decides she can make a better dot than that.
Vashti starts experimenting and paints lots of dots — small dots, bigger dots, dots in many colours (red, yellow, blue, green), dots that overlap to make purple, and huge dots.
Vashti makes one painting that is a dot made by NOT painting a dot — just empty space in the middle.
Vashti's many dot paintings are displayed at a school art show — a whole wall of them.
A little boy comes up to Vashti at the show and says he wishes he could draw like her. He says he can't even draw a straight line with a ruler.
Vashti hands the boy a blank sheet of paper and tells him to show her a line.
With a shaky, trembling pencil, the boy draws a wobbly line.
Vashti looks at his line, hands it back, and says 'Please… sign it.' The story ends with the cycle of encouragement passed on.

record.setting: Vashti's school — specifically her art class, where the story opens with her sitting at an empty desk staring at a blank piece of paper. The story later moves to the art room where her framed dot hangs and the wider school for the art show.
themes: Creative courage — believing you can't do something stops you from starting
Signing your work gives you ownership and turns a mark into art
Passing it on — confidence spreads from person to person
Small beginnings lead to big results

Vashti jabs the paper with a marker and makes a small dot.

Vashti signs her dot in the corner of the page.
The next week, Vashti finds her signed dot hanging in a gold frame above the teacher's desk.

Vashti paints dots in many different sizes and colours including red, yellow, blue, and green.
Some of Vashti's dots overlap to make purple.

A little boy comes up to Vashti at the art show.
The boy says he can't even draw a straight line with a ruler.
Vashti gives the boy a blank sheet of paper.
